title: Efficiency of Glucosamine in Treating Temporomandibular Joint Osteoarthritis: A Meta-Analytic Umbrella Review
research_question: Does glucosamine have any influence in improving the functional parameters of patients with temporomandibular joint osteoarthritis in comparison with any other drugs/placebo?
methods_summary: Search of Medline (PubMed), Web of Science, Scopus, and EMBASE up to September 2023
Screening at title, abstract, and full-text level
Risk of bias assessment using JBI critical appraisal tool
Meta-analysis using random effects model with Comprehensive Meta-Analysis software
Pooled estimate of standardized mean differences for maximum mouth opening and pain reduction
dataset: 4 systematic reviews (De Souza et al., 2012; Melo et al., 2018; Ruiz-Romero et al., 2022; Derwich et al., 2023) with total participants ranging from 39 to 538
key_findings: Glucosamine showed non-significant but discernible effects on maximum mouth opening (SMD = 0.288, p = 0.15)
Glucosamine showed non-significant effect on pain reduction (SMD = 0.217, p = 0.476)
Compared to control groups (ibuprofen and tramadol), glucosamine showed slightly more favorable outcomes
Administration for at least 3 months was associated with significant reduction in TMJ pain and increase in mouth opening
Glucosamine and chondroitin sulfate are safe with lower risk of side effects compared to NSAIDs
extracted_insights: Glucosamine demonstrates a trend toward improvement in TMJ OA functional parameters (mouth opening and pain reduction) but does not reach statistical significance in pooled analysis. The efficacy is influenced by administration duration (minimum 3 months). Glucosamine is a safe adjunctive therapy with fewer side effects than NSAIDs, warranting further longitudinal studies to confirm its clinical effectiveness in TMJ OA management.
